Use the navigation sidebar to browse the available documents.

Search
Use the search box in the sidebar (or press /) to quickly find pages by title or content.

title: Finding Source Code
text: Sometimes, the documentation doesn’t tell you everything you need to know. You need to get elbow-deep in the code

title: Finding Source Code
Finding Source Code
section: Finding the source code for S3 and S4 methods

title: Finding Source Code
section: Finding the source code for R6 methods
text:
Finding the source code for R6 methods
R6 objects are a bit harder because the methods are stored in the object itself. If you have an R6 object, you can use the $ operator and the name of the source just like a simpler function.

library(cmdstanr)
file <- file.path(cmdstan_path(), "examples/bernoulli/bernoulli.stan")
mod  <- cmdstan_model(file)
mod$sample

If you don’t yet have an R6 object, the class name is usually the same as the function that creates it, and although that function may not be exported when you use library(), you can use the ::: operator and find the method under public_methods.

cmdstanr:::CmdStanModel$public_methods$sample

title: Finding Source Code
text:
Finding the One True Source
Functions of course call other functions, and some call on tools that are written in another language or even compiled code. You’ll have to follow that trail of breadcrumbs.

title: Finding Documentation
text:
The first thing to try when you want to find documentation for a function is the ? operator.

?mean


help(mean)

title: Finding Documentation
text:
The first thing to try when you want to find documentation for a function is the ? operator.

?mean


help(mean)

title: Finding Documentation
text:
Finding help for special functions
Some functions have special characters in their names, such as [ or %in%. To find help for these functions, you need to use backticks around the function name.

?`[`


?`[[`


?`%in%`

title: Finding Documentation
section: Finding help for functions in packages
text:
Finding help for functions in packages
If you haven’t loaded the package that contains the function you are interested in, the ? operator may not work.

?st_sf

No documentation for 'st_sf' in specified packages and libraries:
you could try '??st_sf'



?sf::st_sf # or help("st_sf", package = "sf")


??st_sf # or help.search("st_sf")


library(sf)

Linking to GEOS 3.12.1, GDAL 3.8.4, PROJ 9.4.0; sf_use_s2() is TRUE

?st_sf

title: Finding Documentation
section: Finding help for generic S3 and S4 functions
text:
Finding help for generic S3 and S4 functions
Some R functions are generic, meaning they have different methods for different classes of objects. For example, the plot() function has different methods for plotting data frames, time series, and other object types. Running ?plot may give you general information about the function, but may not provide details about the specific method you are interested in.

?plot

Help on topic 'plot' was found in the following packages:

  Package               Library
  sf                    /home/runner/work/_temp/renv/cache/v5/linux-ubuntu-noble/R-4.5/x86_64-pc-linux-gnu/sf/1.1-0/3e61ec188b75e39ce061f6b2dee44179
  graphics              /opt/R/4.5.3/lib/R/library
  base                  /home/runner/.cache/R/renv/sandbox/linux-ubuntu-noble/R-4.5/x86_64-pc-linux-gnu/46e831e3


Using the first match ...

?plot.data.frame # help(plot.data.frame)


?plot.lm

title: Finding Documentation
text:
Finding help for R6 methods
Some R packages use the R6 object-oriented system, which has a different way of defining methods. Use of ? isn’t standardized for these methods, but you can usually at least get into the documentation for the class and then click to the method you’re interested in. For example, the CmdStanModel class in the cmdstanr package has a sample() method for fitting models. You can get help for this method by running:

?CmdStanModel # and then click $sample()

cmdstanr also has shortcuts to get to specific method documentation:

?'model-method-sample'

But the name of this shortcut is not standardized across other packages. Instead, use the help() function:

help("sample", package = "cmdstanr")

title: Finding Documentation
section: Finding help for functions that you wrote
text:
Finding help for functions that you wrote
For the love of salmon, please annotate your code. For short scripts and on-the-fly helper functions, make sure to use comments to explain what the function does, what the inputs are, and what the outputs are. If you are using Positron and your function is defined anywhere in your project directory, you can right-click on the function name and select “Go to Definition” to jump to the code for that function (and the comments should be there too!). You can also use Ctrl+F to search for the function in your file, or Ctrl+Shift+F to search for the function name in your project directory. If you use consistent whitespace rules and unique function names, you can search for function_name <- to separate the function definition from other function references.

title: Debugging with error handling
text:
When writing scripts, you rarely want a future run with new data to crash and emit an error message like:

Warning message: NAs introduced by coercion


[1] NA

The code ran fine before! Did you run the script with a typo in a parameter? Or is there badly formatted data in your new input file? Which of the 10,000 rows has the bad data??
If you’re developing a tool or planning to reuse your script, you can save a lot of headaches in the long-run by writing your code to validate inputs and fail early with bespoke, informative messages.
For instance, say you have a script that simply reads a csv and writes a new one with rounded values from a single column:

round_column <- function(df, col_num_raw, prec_raw) {
  col_num <- as.numeric(col_num_raw)
  prec <- as.numeric(prec_raw)
  df$rounded <- round(as.numeric(df[[col_num]]), digits = prec)
  print(df)
}

You might run this script with a test dataset:

test_data_1 <- data.frame(
  labels = c("A", "B", "C"),
  values = c(10.556, 21.891, 3.14159)
)
print(test_data_1)

  labels   values
1      A 10.55600
2      B 21.89100
3      C  3.14159

round_column(test_data_1, 2, 2)

  labels   values rounded
1      A 10.55600   10.56
2      B 21.89100   21.89
3      C  3.14159    3.14


And celebrate that it works!
But then you try some real data:

test_data_2 <- data.frame(
  labels = c("A", "B", "C"),
  values = c(10.556, 21.891, '3.141S9')
)
print(test_data_2)

  labels  values
1      A  10.556
2      B  21.891
3      C 3.141S9

round_column(test_data_2, 2, 2)

Warning in round_column(test_data_2, 2, 2): NAs introduced by coercion


  labels  values rounded
1      A  10.556   10.56
2      B  21.891   21.89
3      C 3.141S9      NA
